excerpts.name: SKQS abstract
excerpts.text:
《集古錄》•十卷〈通行本〉[编辑]

宋歐陽修撰。修有《詩本義》，已著錄。古人法書惟重真跡。自梁元帝始集錄碑刻之文為《碑英》一百二十卷，見所撰《金樓子》，是為金石文字之祖。今其書不傳。曾鞏欲作《金石錄》而未就，僅制一序存《元豐類稿》中。修始采摭佚逸，積至千卷。撮其大要，各為之說。至嘉祐治平間，修在政府，又各書其卷尾。於是文或小異，蓋隨時有所竄定也。修自書其後，題嘉祐癸卯。至熙寧二年己酉，修季子棐，複摭其略，別為目錄。上距癸卯蓋六年，而棐記稱錄既成之八年，則是錄之成當在嘉祐六年辛丑。其真跡跋尾，則多系治平初年所書，亦間有在熙甯初者，知棐之目錄，固承修之命而為之也。諸碑跋今皆具修集中。其跋自為書，則自宋方崧卿裒聚真跡，刻於廬陵。曾宏父《石刻鋪敍》稱有二百四十六跋，陳振孫《書錄解題》稱有三百五十跋，修子棐所記則曰凡二百九十六跋，修又自雲凡四百餘篇有跋。近日刻《集古錄》者又為之說曰：“世所傳集古跋四百餘篇，而棐乃謂二百九十六。雖是時修尚無恙，然續跋不應多逾百篇，因疑寫本誤以三百為二百。”以今考之，則通此十卷，乃正符四百餘跋之數。蓋以集本與真跡合編，與專據集本者不同。宋時廬陵之刻，今已不傳，無從核定，不必以棐記為疑矣。是書原本但隨得隨錄，不復詮次年月。故修之自序曰：“有卷帙而無時世先後，蓋其取多而未已也。”近來刻本，乃以時代先後為序，而於每卷之末附列原本卷帙次第。轉有年月倒置，更易補正之處，故錢曾《讀書敏求記》以為失其初意。然考毛晉跋是書曰：“自序謂上自周穆王以來，則當以吉曰癸巳石刻為卷首。毛伯敦三銘是作序目後所得，宜在卷末，即子棐亦未敢妄為詮次。蓋周益公未能考訂”云云。據此，則周必大時之本已案時世為次，其由來固已久矣。今刻修文集者，但序時代，不復存每卷末之原次，則益為疏耳。今仍依見行篇次著於錄焉。

**_Ji Gu Lu_ (Collected Records of Antiquity) • Ten Juan (Common Edition) [Edit]**

Authored by Ouyang Xiu of the Song dynasty. Xiu also authored the _Shi Ben Yi_ (Original Meaning of the Odes), which has already been cataloged. For ancient calligraphic works, only original pieces were valued. Beginning with Emperor Yuan of Liang, [he] collected texts from stele inscriptions into the _Bei Ying_ (Stele Blossoms) in 120 juan, as mentioned in his _Jin Lou Zi_ (Master of the Golden Tower); this is considered the progenitor of epigraphic collections. Today, that book is no longer extant. Zeng Gong wished to create a _Jinshi Lu_ (Record of Metal and Stone) but did not complete it; only a preface he composed survives in the _Yuanfeng Lei Gao_ (Classified Drafts of the Yuanfeng Era).

Xiu began collecting lost and scattered items, eventually accumulating up to a thousand juan [of materials]. He extracted their main points and wrote commentaries for each. During the Jiayou and Zhiping reigns [1056-1067], while Xiu was serving in the government, he also wrote colophons at the end of each scroll. Consequently, the texts [of the colophons] sometimes differ slightly, likely because he made revisions over time. Xiu himself wrote a postface, dating it to the _guimao_ year of the Jiayou reign [1063]. By the second year of the Xining reign, the _jiyou_ year [1069], Xiu's youngest son, Fei, again extracted the essential points and separately created a catalog. This was approximately six years after the _guimao_ year [1063], but Fei's record states it was eight years after the main collection was completed; therefore, the completion of the main collection should have been in the sixth year of Jiayou, the _xinchou_ year [1061]. The original colophons were mostly written in the early years of the Zhiping reign [c. 1064-1065], though some are occasionally dated to the early Xining years [c. 1068-]. From this, we know that Fei's catalog was certainly undertaken under Xiu's instruction.

These various stele colophons are now all included in Xiu's collected works. As for the colophons forming a separate book, this originated with Fang Songqing of the Song, who gathered the original colophons and had them engraved in Luling. Zeng Hongfu's _Shi Ke Pu Xu_ states there were 246 colophons; Chen Zhensun's _Shu Lu Jie Ti_ states 350; Xiu's son Fei recorded a total of 296; Xiu himself also stated there were over 400 pieces with colophons. Recent engravers of the _Ji Gu Lu_ have also offered an explanation: "The commonly transmitted _Ji Gu_ colophons number over 400, yet Fei stated 296. Although Xiu was still alive at that time, the supplementary colophons should not have exceeded one hundred. Therefore, it is suspected that copyists mistakenly wrote 'two hundred' instead of 'three hundred' [in Fei's record]." Examining it now, throughout these ten juan [of the current edition], the number precisely matches the figure of over 400 colophons. This is likely because [this edition] combines the collected version with the original colophons [as separately compiled/engraved], differing from versions based solely on the [original] collected version. The Luling engraving from the Song dynasty is no longer extant, so there is no way to verify this; there is no need to doubt Fei's record [regarding the number _he_ recorded].

The original version of this book simply recorded items as they were acquired and did not arrange them chronologically. Therefore, Xiu's own preface states: "There are volumes, but without chronological order, likely because the collection was large and still ongoing." Recent engraved editions arrange the items chronologically, appending the original volume sequence at the end of each volume. This, in turn, leads to instances where dates are inverted or require correction, which is why Qian Zeng, in his _Dushu Minqiu Ji_, considered it to have lost the original intention. However, examining Mao Jin's colophon to this book, it states: "The preface mentions [going back] to King Mu of Zhou, so the _Ji Ri Gui Si_ stone inscription should be at the head of the volume. The three inscriptions of Mao Bodun were obtained after the preface and catalog were created and belong at the end; even [Ouyang Xiu's] son Fei did not dare to arrange them recklessly, likely because Zhou Yigong [Zhou Bida] had not been able to research and establish [the correct order]" and so forth. Based on this, the version from Zhou Bida's time was already arranged chronologically; its origin [the chronological arrangement] is certainly long-standing. Those who now engrave Xiu's collected works only arrange by period and no longer preserve the original sequence at the end of each volume; this is even more negligent.

We now follow the sequence of entries in the currently circulating edition for this catalog entry.

excerpts.name: Preface Ouyang Xiu
excerpts.text:
SKQS
　集古録序 物常聚於所好，而常得於有力之彊。有力而不好，好之而無力，雖近且易，有不能致之。象、犀、虎、豹，蠻夷、山海、殺人之獸，然其齒角皮革可聚而有也；玉出崑崙、流沙，萬里之外，經十餘譯，乃至乎中國；珠出南海，常生深淵，採者腰絙而入水，形色非人，往往不出，則下飽蛟魚；金礦於山，鑿深而穴逺，篝火餱糧而後進，其崖崩窟塞，則遂葬於其中者，率常數十百人。其逺且難，而又多死禍，常如此。然而金、玉、珠、璣，世常兼聚而有也。凡物，好之而有力，則無不至也。湯盤、孔鼎、岐陽之鼓、岱山、鄒嶧、㑹稽之刻石，與夫漢魏已來聖君賢士桓碑、彛器、銘、詩、序、記，下至古文、籀、篆、分、𨽻、諸家之字書，皆三代以來至寶、怪竒、偉麗、工妙、可喜之物。其去人不逺，其取之無禍，然而風霜、兵火，湮淪磨滅，散棄於山崖、墟莽之間，未嘗収拾者，由世之好者少也。幸而有好之者，又其力或不足，故僅得其一二而不能使其聚也。夫力莫如好，好莫如一。予性顓而嗜古，凡世人之所貪者，皆無欲於其間，故得一其所好於斯。好之已篤，則力雖未足，猶能致之。故上自周穆王以來，下更秦、漢、隋、唐、五代，外至四海、九州、名山、大澤、窮崖、絶谷、荒林、破塚、神仙、鬼物、詭怪所傳，莫不皆有，以為集古録。以謂轉〈一作傳〉寫失眞，故因其石本軸而藏之。有卷帙次第，而無時世之先後，葢其取多而未巳，故隨其所得而録之。又以謂聚多而終必散，乃撮其大要，别為録目，因并載夫可與史傳正其闕謬者，以傳後學，庶益於多聞。或譏予曰：「物多則其勢難聚，聚久而無不散，何必區區於是哉？」予對曰：「足吾所好，玩而老焉，可也。象、犀、金、玉之聚，其能果不散乎？予固未能以此而易彼也。」廬陵歐陽修序。

在昔洛陽與余遊者，皆一時豪儁之士也。而陳郡謝希深善評文章，河南尹師魯辨論精博。余每有所作，二人者必伸紙疾讀，便得余深意，以示他人。亦或時有所稱，皆非余所自得者也。宛陵梅聖俞，善人君子也。與余共處窮約，每見余小有可喜事，懽然若在諸已。自三君之亡，余亦老且病矣。此叙之作，既無謝、尹之知音；而集録成書，恨聖俞之不見也。悲夫！嘉祐八年歲在癸夘七月二十四日書。

**Preface to the _Collection of Ancient Records_ (_Ji Gu Lu_)**

Objects tend to gather where they are liked, and are often acquired by those with power and strength. If one has power but no interest, or has interest but lacks power, even things close by and easily obtained may prove unattainable. Consider elephants, rhinoceroses, tigers, and leopards – beasts from barbarian lands, mountains, and seas that can kill humans; yet their tusks, horns, and hides can be gathered and possessed. Jade comes from Kunlun and the Shifting Sands, tens of thousands of miles away, passing through more than ten intermediaries before reaching China. Pearls come from the South China Sea, typically growing in deep abysses; collectors tie ropes around their waists and enter the water, appearing inhuman, and often do not return, becoming food for sea serpents and fish below. Gold is mined in mountains, requiring deep shafts and distant tunnels; miners advance with torches and provisions, and when cliffs collapse or tunnels cave in, often dozens or hundreds are buried within. Acquiring these things is distant and difficult, often involving death and disaster. Nevertheless, gold, jade, pearls, and gems are constantly gathered and possessed in the world. Generally, if one likes an object and has the means, nothing is unattainable.

King Tang's Basin, Confucius's Tripod, the Stone Drums of Qiyang, the inscriptions of Mount Tai, Mount Zouyi, and Mount Kuaiji, as well as the steles (bei), ritual vessels (yiqi), inscriptions (ming), poems (shi), prefaces (xu), and records (ji) of sage rulers and worthy officials since the Han and Wei dynasties, down to various calligraphic scripts like ancient script (guwen), Greater Seal (zhouzhuan), Clerical subtypes (fen, li), and others – these are all most precious treasures from the Three Dynasties onward, strange and marvelous, magnificent and beautiful, skillfully crafted, and delightful objects. They are not far removed from people, and acquiring them involves no disaster. Yet, due to wind and frost, warfare and fire, neglect and erosion, they are scattered and abandoned among mountain cliffs, ruins, and wilderness, having never been collected systematically. This is because few people in the world appreciate them. Even if, fortunately, there are those who appreciate them, their resources may be insufficient, so they obtain only one or two items and cannot make them gather into a collection.

Now, power is not as good as fondness, and fondness is not as good as singular focus. My nature is single-minded, and I am addicted to antiquity. I have no desire for those things coveted by worldly people. Therefore, I can focus my interest solely on this. Once this fondness became deep, even though my resources were insufficient, I was still able to acquire things. Thus, [my collection includes items] from as early as King Mu of Zhou, down through the Qin, Han, Sui, Tang, and Five Dynasties; geographically, it extends to the Four Seas and Nine Provinces, famous mountains and great marshes, remote cliffs and secluded valleys, desolate forests and ruined tombs, [including inscriptions related to] spirits, immortals, ghosts, and strange legends. There is almost nothing unrepresented. I have compiled these into the _Collection of Ancient Records_ (_Ji Gu Lu_). Believing that hand-copying leads to loss of authenticity (variant: transmission through copying), I have preserved them based on the original stone rubbings mounted on scrolls. There are volumes and sequential order, but no arrangement according to historical period, likely because the collection grew large before completion, so items were recorded as they were obtained. Furthermore, believing that large collections ultimately scatter, I extracted the main points to create a separate catalog (_Lu Mu_). In it, I also included material that can correct deficiencies and errors in the official histories, in order to transmit it to later scholars, hopefully contributing to broader knowledge.

Someone might criticize me, saying: "When things are numerous, they are difficult to gather; collections, after a long time, invariably disperse. Why trouble yourself with such trivialities?" I reply: "It is enough to satisfy my interest and amuse myself into old age. Can collections of ivory, rhino horn, gold, and jade truly avoid dispersal? I am certainly unwilling to trade my pursuit for theirs."

Preface by Ouyang Xiu of Luling.

**[Addendum]**

In the past, those who associated with me in Luoyang were all outstanding men of the time. Xie Xishen [Xie Jiang] of Chen Commandery was skilled at evaluating literature, and Yin Shilu [Yin Zhu] of Henan was precise and profound in debate. Whenever I wrote something, these two would immediately spread the paper and read rapidly, grasping my deeper meaning to explain it to others. Any praise my work occasionally received often came indirectly through them. Mei Shengyu [Mei Yaochen] of Wanling was a good man and a gentleman. He shared periods of poverty and hardship with me, and whenever I had some small, pleasing success, he would rejoice as if it were his own. Since these three gentlemen passed away, I too have become old and ill. Now that this preface is written, there are no connoisseurs like Xie and Yin; and as the collected records are compiled into a book, I lament that Shengyu cannot see it. How sad!

Written on the 24th day of the 7th month of the Gui Mao year, the 8th year of the Jiayou reign [1063 CE].
